display_title: Fiscal Governance Reform in Nigeria: Lessons form the State Fiscal Transparency, Accountability and Sustainability.
abstract:
This learning review looks beyond the specific parameters of the State Fiscal Transparency, Accountability and Sustainability (SFTAS) operational design to reflect candidly on achievements and challenges and to better understand the pathways, impact, and sustainability of the results. Its focus is two-fold. First, it examines what
improvements were achieved and what impact this had. In doing so, it looks beyond
whether or not the disbursement-linked indicators (DLIs) were achieved to analyze: the nature of the fiscal governance rules, standards and practices that were adopted; the extent of their impact on the quality and accountability of public financial management and fiscal outcomes; and the sustainability of reforms. Second, it looks at how and why improvements in fiscal governance were achieved through an examination of the different factors that help explain why some changes were more sustained and some states performed better than others. The review is structured as follows: Section I provides the country and fiscal context in which the Program was introduced as well as the objectives of the SFTAS Program for Results (PforR) and its key design features. Section II unpacks the results by key results areas (KRA) both in terms of DLI achievements and the impacts and sustainability of reforms. Section III explores how economic, political, institutional, normative and process factors contribute to state performance and explain patterns and variations. Section IV concludes.

theme: NA
display_title: Nepal - Public Financial Management Multi-donor Trust Fund Progress Report : January - June 2025
abstract: The Government of Nepal, with the public financial management multi-donor trust fund (PFM MDTF) support, has finalized the National Public Financial Management Reform Strategy 2025-2030, a flagship reform agenda aimed at strengthening the credibility, efficiency, and transparency of Nepal’s PFM systems. The Strategy builds on findings from the third federal Public Expenditure Financial Accountability (PEFA) Assessment completed in April 2024, which provided an evidence base for identifying reform priorities. The Strategy development process was notably inclusive and participatory, involving more than 700 stakeholders from all tiers of government - federal, provincial, and local - through regional consultations, thematic workshops, and field missions to Madhesh, Gandaki, and Koshi provinces. Structured around seven strategic pillars, ranging from budget reliability and execution to fiscal risk management, intergovernmental fiscal transfers, and external scrutiny, and three cross-cutting pillars, covering capacity, information and communication technology (ICT) and climate and gender-responsive PFM - the Strategy outlines a comprehensive and ambitious roadmap for strengthening PFM systems at all levels of government. Each pillar is anchored in specific, results-oriented objectives, including improving macro-fiscal forecasting, streamlining procurement, modernizing revenue systems, enhancing debt management, and institutionalizing asset oversight. The Strategy represents a major milestone in Nepal’s commitment to sound public financial governance and provides a solid platform for continued PFM MDTF engagement and support in the years ahead.

display_title: Rwanda - Public Finance Management Reform Project
abstract: Ratings for the Public Finance Management (PFM) Reform Project for Rwanda were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: given the complexity of migrating to International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) accrual, a phased approach with gradual adoption and enhanced training is necessary for a smooth transition. Having comprehensive asset valuation frameworks and strategies as well as robust information technology (IT) systems to ensure accurate, timely, and sustainable financial reporting is critical for the transition to ISPAS accrual. The uptake of PFM reforms by sector ministries requires extensive sensitization and awareness campaigns. The adoption and optimization of the PFM IT systems require sustained capacity-building efforts to ensure its effective utilization by government entities. A well-structured institutional arrangement is instrumental for a coordinated approach in achieving results. Sustainable capacity building in the public sector requires not only the institutionalization of training programs but also the financial sustainability of these interventions.

theme: NA
display_title: FinHealth Togo : Tackling Public Financial Management and Broader Governance Reforms to Improve Health Service Delivery
abstract: The universal health coverage agenda in Togo will increasingly rely on government funding, which makes the quality of the PFM system crucial to achieve gains of the wider health sector agenda. Available data suggest that Togo is undergoing a health financing transition with a slow but gradual increase in domestic general government health expenditure per capita. Over the years, the government has established several free and subsidized healthcare programs targeting vulnerable groups. These programs are expected to be folded into a social protection package as part of the universal health insurance launched in January 2024. With the expected increase of domestic government financing, it is vital to integrate PFM into the health systems strengthening agenda and promote greater harmony between PFM systems and health financing strategies. This study uses the World Bank’s FinHealth toolkit to undertake an exploration of service delivery bottlenecks in health that are known to have an impact in terms of access to healthcare services and universal healthcare coverage (UHC). Building on the wealth of microdata and knowledge generated in the sector in recent years, the study adopted a mixed-methods approach combining desk review, data collection, and analysis. This approach provides an opportunity to conduct a more systematic assessment of three critical service delivery challenges, shortages of essential health products, unavailability of medical equipment and maintenance, and a lack of qualified personnel in health facilities, and establish a causal relationship between various governance issues (PFM and non-PFM) and health service delivery outcomes.

theme: Governance and Public Sector Management,Finance,Develop Financial Markets,Public Finance Management,Climate Change and Sustainable Finance,Domestic Revenue Administration
display_title:
Prosperity Unearthed : Wealth-Sharing Mechanisms for Peace and Equitable Growth in the
Middle East and North Africa
abstract:
This report examines the causes of different development outcomes and
suggests ways to promote inclusive prosperity and peace. It does so by looking
at wealth sharing through three prisms: time, space, and fragility and conflict.
More specifically, the report assesses how policy makers in the MENA region
have approached the sharing of resources across time (for example, saving
versus spending) and across geographical space (for example, concentrating
versus distributing). The report argues that deviations from good practices in
these two dimensions help explain some of the region’s unfulfilled development
promise, including in countries affected by fragility, conflict, and violence
(FCV). Based on this assessment, the report identifies opportunities to leverage
hydrocarbon resources to build sustainable and inclusive prosperity. The report
focuses on wealth sharing and emphasizes the crucial role that inclusive
economic policies, robust transparency arrangements, and strong, capable
institutions play in providing services, infrastructure, and equitable transfers
financed by resource wealth.

display_title: Liberia - Public Financial Management Reforms for Institutional Strengthening Project
abstract: Ratings for Public Financial Management (PFM) Reforms for Institutional Strengthening Project for Liberia were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: a key lesson from the Project is about the importance of government commitment for successful implementation of PFM reforms like any other reforms. Indicators designed to capture outcomes rather than outputs are necessary to ensure the achievements of projects are measured beyond merely the activities. Another key lesson is that projects should have sufficient buffers and contingencies to address the impact of unexpected externalities. Another lesson is the importance of stakeholder alignment and stakeholder buy-in.

display_title:
Demonstrating the Value of Digital Transformation Solutions : Viet Nam’s Flood Exposure
Reporting and Decision-Support Platform (FEDS) - Case Study Note
abstract: This case study note describes how a use case was developed to address flood risks in a major city and was ultimately adopted by Ho Chi Minh City. Viet Nam’s Flood Exposure Reporting and Decision-Support Platform (FEDS) is an example of a simple yet powerful digital tool that addresses Public Asset Governance (PAG) problems in Viet Nam. It is the first such tool developed as a use case under the Disruptive Technologies for Public Asset Governance (DT4PAG) program, supported by the World Bank and Switzerland’s State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO). FEDS provides a valuable platform for the government, and eventually, citizens, to report flood incidents in a way that supports improved decision-making on flood management, as well as investments in flood mitigation. This note shows how cost-effective, easy-to-use digital solutions can contribute to increased government effectiveness and quality of service delivery. The note provides an overview of platform objectives, the challenges encountered, the solutions applied, and some critical success factors and lessons learned in the Viet Nam context. Viet Nam’s Flood Exposure Reporting and Decision-Support Platform (FEDS) was developed to demonstrate the value of disruptive technologies for public asset governance, focusing on improving flood reporting and management in Ho Chi Minh City (HCMC), the largest city in Viet Nam. It was identified as a priority need by the HCMC Department of Information and Communications (DIC), Department of Construction (DOC), and the HCMC Infrastructure Management Center (HIMC), as it aligns with their digital transformation vision and program and addresses a pressing need of the city. The FEDS use case was developed under the Disruptive Technologies for Public Asset Governance (DT4PAG) program, with support from the World Bank and Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO). This note traces the development and ultimate adoption and deployment of FEDS in 21 districts and the Thu Duc City district of HCMC. This note documents the context, approaches, and solutions deployed, with particular reference to the three DT4PAG pillars of people, process, and technology (PPT), and also assesses the results achieved and lessons to be considered in similar efforts. The DT4PAG program is currently supporting agencies to scale the use of FEDS and to incorporate its outputs into broader flood-management decision-making in the city.

theme: NA
display_title: Reforming Justice - Benchmarking Judicial Effectiveness through the JUPITER Assessment
abstract:
This Brief introduces the Justice Pillars Towards Evidence-Based Reform (JUPITER)
initiative, a standardized yet flexible, country-based assessment tool that evaluates
the effectiveness of justice systems across countries. Developed by the World
Bank’s Global Program on Justice and the Rule of Law, JUPITER aims at identifying
strengths and weaknesses in key pillars of judicial performance—access, efficiency,
and quality. The Brief lays out key advantages of JUPITER’s comprehensive
approach: its focus on areas with an empirical link to outcomes as investigated in
rigorous academic research; its emphasis on the law as well as its application, which
enables the identification of potential implementation gaps; its assessment of both
the formal and customary justice institutions; and its coverage of the entire country.
In so doing, we bring examples from its application in some of the poorest and most
fragile countries in the world such as Liberia and South Sudan. This Brief explains the
significance of access, efficiency, and quality for the effectiveness of justice delivery
and how JUPITER measures performance in each of those areas. It concludes by
discussing how the methodology could enable cross-country comparisons as more
data is gathered through the JUPITER assessment

authors: Abdellatif Ahmed, Lobna Mohamed;Andrews, Donna;Kambole, Kutemba;Mbulo, Mwila;Venkateswaran, Rama Krishnan
theme: FY17 - Fiscal Policy,FY17 - Judicial and other Dispute Resolution Mechanisms,FY17 - Rule of Law,FY17 - Public Expenditure Policy
display_title: Zambia - Assessment of Access to Justice and Alternative Dispute Resolution Systems
abstract: The World Bank (WB)-supported Public Expenditure and Institutional Review of the Zambian Judicial Sector (PIER) of the Zambian Judicial Sector (WB, 2022) analyzed access to justice from the supply side only, making recommendations to streamline processes and procedures within the Zambian courts to alleviate access challenges. Therefore, a complementary study was needed to assess the following two areas: 1) demand side aspects of access to justice, especially in under-served areas of Zambia; and 2) Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) processes and systems in Zambia (and their potential to support the overburdened formal court system). The Assessment contains four substantive components (Chapters 2–5). Chapter 2 comprises the access to justice component of this Assessment, and Chapter 3 the ADR component. Chapter 4 proposes near-to-medium term recommendations to improve the access to justice situation in underserved communities and enhance ADR uptake. Chapter 5 examines the Ministry of Justice (MoJ)’s new strategic plan (SP) (2022–2026) (MoJ, 2023) through the lens of the PEIR and this current assessment.

theme: NA
display_title: Reforming Justice - Balancing Efficiency and Accessibility through Judicial Map Reform
abstract:
Judicial map reforms refer to an emerging stream of reform programs that
attempts to improve the responsiveness of the justice system by adjusting the
courts’ location, size, and jurisdiction to demographic and socio-economic factors.
Building on the World Bank’s experience in supporting client countries reform their
judicial maps, this Brief lays out a framework for the design and implementation of
such programs. It explains why countries attempt to revise their judicial maps, how
they do it, and what types of policy interventions it entails. It also highlights the
significance of judicial and demographic data, the need for clearly defined criteria,
and the importance of communication and impact evaluation for the successful
implementation of reforms. The Brief shows how countries that struggle to deliver
effective justice across their territories can lay their court networks in a way that
maximizes reach with limited resources. It also discusses the growing role digital
solutions could play in judicial map reforms

theme: NA
display_title: Reforming Justice - Fostering Engagement Between  Statutory Courts and Customary Law Systems
abstract:
This Brief examines the complex relationship between statutory courts and
customary law systems, highlighting the historical context that fostered legal
pluralism in many countries. It argues that attempts to replace customary
practices with uniform legal frameworks have largely failed, suggesting instead
that a harmonious coexistence of these systems can enhance access to justice and
fairness. The Brief identifies practical mechanisms for engagement, emphasizing
the need for context-sensitive approaches that recognize the ingrained nature
of customary systems, especially in regions where they are preferred for their
accessibility and cultural relevance. The Brief highlights several factors that should
be considered when designing engagement, including state recognition; jurisdiction;
articulation and documentation of customary law; referral pathways; appeal
pathways; and oversight and accountability. The Brief also explores strategies
for addressing harmful practices, as customary justice systems can incur human
rights violations, including discrimination against women and marginalized groups,
denial of fair trial rights, and the enforcement of harmful traditional punishments.

theme: NA
display_title: Reforming Justice - Increasing Access Through Small Claim Courts
abstract: Small claims courts and procedures play an important role in enhancing the efficiency and accessibility of the justice system. When well-designed, they can significantly streamline judicial processes, reduce the burden on courts, and increase access to justice for individuals and small businesses. This brief presents trends in the use of such mechanisms. It systematically analyzes key components essential for designing effective small claims systems, including the court structure, the nature of the proceedings (mandatory vs. optional), monetary thresholds for claims, filing processes, fee structures, timelines, and the admissibility of evidence. The brief concludes with recommendations for jurisdictions to assess and refine their small claims systems based on empirical data and user feedback, ensuring they effectively address the barriers to justice faced by litigants.

theme: NA
display_title: Supporting Access to Justice in South Sudan - A 2025 JUPITER Assessment
abstract: This report assesses the state and performance of the justice system of South Sudan through the JUPITER methodology. JUPITER uses qualitative and quantitative data to evaluate both de jure and de facto justice delivery metrics, identifying gaps in the legal framework and its implementation and creating comparable metrics to benchmark against similar indicators and other economies. The study focuses on the system’s effectiveness in service delivery across three areas: Access to Justice, Efficiency, and Quality. JUPITER aims to identify strengths and areas of improvement in these key areas, serving as a basis for policy dialogue.

abstract: Această secțiune a Documentației de Atribuire include ansamblul cerințelor pe baza cărora fiecare Ofertant va elabora Oferta (Propunerea Tehnică și Propunerea Financiară) pentru realizarea serviciilor care fac obiectul Contractului ce rezultă din această procedură. În cadrul acestei proceduri, Secretariatul General al Guvernului (SGG) îndeplinește rolul de Autoritate Contractantă, respectiv Achizitor în cadrul Contractului. Pentru scopul prezentei secțiuni a Documentației de Atribuire, orice activitate descrisă într-un anumit capitol din Caietul de Sarcini și nespecificată explicit în alt capitol, trebuie interpretată ca fiind menționată în toate capitolele unde se consideră de către Ofertant că aceasta trebuia menționată pentru asigurarea îndeplinirii obiectului Contractului.

theme: GovTech Enabling Environment,Governance and Public Sector Management,GovTech,Public Service Delivery,Core Government Systems
display_title: E-Government Assessment in the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina - Rapid Assessment Results
abstract: This assessment aims to inform a roadmap for FBiH's digital transformation to enhance public service delivery. It evaluates the current state of key digital and non-digital enablers of e-government (strategies and policies, laws and regulations, institutions and coordination, Information and Communication Technology (ICT) and e-Government infrastructure, and capacity and resources) and offers recommendations and guiding principles from similar reforms. The methodology employed combines semi-structured interviews, desk research, and a survey conducted in collaboration with FBiH authorities. This holistic approach ensures a thorough understanding of existing challenges and opportunities that would ultimately inform the effective digital transformation of FBiH, aligned with European Union (EU) standards and digital transformation goals1. The assessment, driven by client demand, primarily targets policymakers at the FBiH level and aims to provide a roadmap for digitalizing government services, offering targeted recommendations on e-government aspects.

theme: FY17 - Public Expenditure Management,FY17 - Public Administration,FY17 - Public Finance Management,FY17 - Transparency, Accountability and Good Governance
display_title: 2024 Global Report on the Use of Accrual Accounting for Fiscal Management
abstract: The report presents an analysis of the adoption and implementation of accrual accounting in the public sector to explore its role in providing information on fiscal sustainability, informing economic analysis, and supporting policy decision-making. The analysis examines the benefits and disadvantages of accrual accounting using various methods, including profiling the socioeconomic characteristics of countries and the results of Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability assessments by their accounting basis in 2020; analyzing completeness and comparability issues with partial accrual accounting; reviewing research on accrual-based financial information as an indicator of fiscal risks; and assessing the use of accrual-based financial information for debt management and transparency. The analysis also reviewed the role of technology in facilitating access to accrual data and the costs versus benefits of accrual adoption. It also included three case studies of developing countries preparing to transition to full accrual accounting. Drawing on the lessons learned by countries that have successfully adopted accrual accounting, this report outlines a ‘theory of change’ that identifies two distinct phases of accrual accounting reforms. The first phase involves the initial adoption of accrual accounting and the preparation of an opening balance sheet, as well as the production of accrual-based financial statements for individual public sector entities and consolidated financial statements for the whole of government. This phase primarily focuses on improving transparency. However, it does not necessarily lead to better decision-making as it only provides information about past financial position and performance. The second phase, which is crucial for achieving high-level outcomes, involves implementing accrual-based planning and budgeting and a performance measurement system. This phase establishes a clear link between accrual-based financial information and financial and nonfinancial performance information, enabling a fully integrated strategic planning, budgeting, financial reporting, and fiscal management approach.

display_title: Ghana - Public Sector Reform for Results Project
abstract: Ratings for the Public Sector Reform for Results Project for Ghana were as follows: outcome and Bank performance were moderately unsatisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: project design should include sufficient lead time for implementation and anticipate delays that the Bank does not have control over. The project was later affected by implementation delays resulting from delays in effectiveness and the impact of an election. Additionally, procurement delay is widely known as a problem across the portfolio. These problems can have been anticipated and measures built in to ensure lower impact on project implementation and outcomes. Secondly, selection of participating entities should be undertaken systematically within a strong methodology that ensures that only entities that are ready to implement are included. Thirdly, Task Team should be realistic an introduce restructuring as soon as it is clear that there are implementation challenges related to the design on the reform context. Fourth, Project Preparation Advance (PPAs) can be strategically used to strengthen project preparation and lay the foundations for strong implementation.

display_title: Overview of the new Infrastructure Governance Assessment Framework (InfraGov 2.0)
abstract: With generous financial support from the Government of Japan under the Quality Infrastructure Investment (QII) Program, the revised Infrastructure Governance Assessment Framework (InfraGov 2.0) presents an updated and integrated diagnostic tool for assessing public investment management (PIM), infrastructure governance, and public asset governance. Designed as a standardized, modular, and practical tool for future assessments of PIM and infrastructure governance, it builds upon the original World Bank’s 8 “Must-Have” functions of PIM and InfraGov framework (InfraGov 1.0) and incorporates lessons learned from a decade of applying various PIM and InfraGov assessment tools including the WB 8 “Must-Haves”, PEFA PIM, IMF PIMA, and OECD Infrastructure Governance.

display_title: Romania - Acord Rambursabil de Servicii de Consultanță Privind Consolidarea Capacităților pentru o Abordare Integrată a Dezvoltării Durabile : Livrabilul 4
abstract: Guvernul României (GdR) s-a angajat, prin Planul național de redresare și reziliență (PNRR), să consolideze coordonarea eforturilor de dezvoltare durabilă (SD) în cadrul administrației publice românești. În cadrul Planului național de redresare și reziliență (PNRR), componenta 14 privind buna guvernare vizează consolidarea coordonării la nivelul guvernului printr-o abordare integrată și coerentă a schimbărilor climatice și a inițiativelor de dezvoltare durabilă. Acest raport corespunde rezultatului 4 al componentei 1: (a) raport care conține o propunere de curriculum pentru un program de dezvoltare a carierei în domeniul dezvoltării durabile, inclusiv contribuții privind opțiunile de formare suplimentare disponibile; și (b) raport privind dezvoltarea programului de formare inițială pentru formatorii în domeniul dezvoltării durabile. Raportul descrie obiectivele, conținutul și structura curriculumului pentru formatorii SD și identifică exemple naționale și internaționale de formare complementară.

display_title: حالة تنفيذ قانون المشتريات العامة اللبناني 244-2021 وتقييم فجوات المهارات والاحتياجات التدريبية : تقرير موجز
abstract: يعمل لبنان على إصلاح نظام المشتريات العامة منذ عام 2019. يعد إصلاح المشتريات العامة التزاما رئيسيا للحكومة اللبنانية للإصلاحات المالية الهيكلية التي تم إجراؤها في المؤتمر الاقتصادي للتنمية والإصلاحات والأعمال (سيدر) في عام 2018، وفي البيانات الوزارية (2019-2021) وفي الاتفاقية على مستوى الموظفين مع صندوق النقد الدولي (2022). وفقا لمنهجية تقييم أنظمة المشتريات (MAPS) التي يدعمها البنك الدولي، صدق لبنان على PPL 244/2021 في يوليو/تموز 2021. دخل القانون حيز التنفيذ في يوليو 2022. بالتزامن مع ذلك، وافق مجلس الوزراء في مايو 2022 على الاستراتيجية الوطنية لإصلاح المشتريات العامة وخطة عملها للفترة 2022-2024. يعرض هذا التقرير الموجز نتائج استطلاع نوعي أجراه معهد المالية باسل فليحان (IOF) في عام 2023 ، بالتعاون مع Insights Analytica ، لتقييم تطبيق قانون المشتريات العامة اللبناني (PPL 244-2021) ، بعد 15 شهرا من دخوله حيز التنفيذ. ويهدف إلى تحديد حالة تنفيذ قانون حماية الأشخاص 244/2021 وتقييم فجوات المهارات والاحتياجات التدريبية المحددة للجهات المشترية على المستويين المركزي والمحلي للحكومة من أجل عملية احتراف مستنيرة. كما يقترح توصيات لمساعدة لبنان في تحسين ممارسات التنفيذ والتدريب وبناء القدرات، بما يتماشى مع PPL 244-2021 والرؤية الاستراتيجية للإصلاح. تقدم نتائج الاستطلاع نظرة ثاقبة حول فعالية التنفيذ القانوني والمؤسسي لقانون حماية الأشخاص 244-2021، وتسليط الضوء على التحديات الداخلية والخارجية، فضلا عن الاحتياجات التدريبية عبر الجهات المشترية واقتراح توصيات نحو التنفيذ الفعال لأحكام PPL 244/2021.

theme: NA
display_title: Reforming Justice - Engaging with Countries on Judicial Budgets
abstract: Adequate budgeting of the judiciary is critical for the effective delivery of justice. This brief presents trends in judicial budgets. Courts account for less than 2 percent of total government expenditure on average, with significant portions dedicated to fixed costs such as salaries, thereby limiting investments in technological advancements essential for improving judicial efficiency. This brief identifies three major trends in judicial budgeting reforms: increasing autonomy for the judiciary in budget management, accountability for budget execution at the court level, and a shift toward performance-based budgeting models. These reforms aim to bolster the judiciary's operational efficiency and public perception, ultimately linking adequate judicial funding to enhanced social and economic outcomes. The findings emphasize the necessity for judicial leaders to continuously advocate for resources by demonstrating the judiciary’s integral role in maintaining law and order, promoting democracy, and fostering societal stability.

display_title: Accès à l’Information et Engagement Citoyen dans la Gestion des Affaires Publiques au Cameroun
abstract: Le Partenariat pour un Gouvernement Ouvert (PGO) est une initiative multilatérale lancée en 2011 pour promouvoir l'ouverture des gouvernements. L'initiative repose sur des principes qui promeuvent la transparence et l'ouverture à de nouvelles formes de concertation, la redevabilité et la collaboration avec la société civile. Le PGO est basé sur l'idée qu'un gouvernement ouvert est plus accessible, réactif et responsable envers les citoyens, et que l'amélioration de la relation entre les citoyens et leur gouvernement présente des avantages à long terme pour l'ensemble de la société. Les gouvernements s'associent à la société civile (SC) pour favoriser la transparence, renforcer le pouvoir des citoyens, lutter contre la corruption et améliorer leur gouvernance, notamment grâce à l'utilisation des nouvelles technologies. Ce modèle unique d'engagement citoyen vise à garantir que les OSC jouent un rôle dans la formulation et le contrôle citoyen de l'action gouvernementale. L'AAI et l'EC sont parmi les quatre critères d'adhésion au Partenariat pour un Gouvernement Ouvert (PGO). Le Cameroun est intéressé à devenir membre du PGO mais ne répond pas encore aux critères requis. Ce diagnostic vise à soutenir les réformes entreprises par le gouvernement pour améliorer son éligibilité au PGO. Le rapport AAI et EC analyse les forces et les faiblesses du gouvernement ouvert dans ces deux domaines.

theme: NA
display_title: Nepal - Public Financial Management Multi-donor Trust Fund Progress Report : July - December 2024
abstract: Strengthening public financial management (PFM) represents a critical aspect of the Government of Nepal’s (GON) strategy to improve governance, optimize the utilization of public resources, and promote inclusive development. There has been increasing interest from civil society organizations, media, the public, and development partners (DPs) in strengthening PFM to enhance the use of public resources and achieve value for money. To this end, the GON has been working closely with DPs to design and implement PFM reform initiatives at the country and sector levels. In response to the GON’s and DPs’ call for a coordinated approach to address PFM challenges, the Nepal PFM Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF) was established to harmonize DPs’ support for PFM reforms. The MDTF has supported investment projects and analytical activities to strengthen PFM in Nepal and is scheduled to close on January 31, 2028.

theme: NA
display_title: Reforming Justice - Improving Service Delivery Through Technology
abstract: This note explores the use of technology to enhance the efficiency, transparency, and accessibility of judicial service delivery. It examines how technological solutions can support streamlining internal court processes, facilitate access for court users, and support the quality of judicial decisions through legal drafting and research tools. The note also emphasizes the importance of framing judicial digitalization within the broader context of public service modernization. This approach goes beyond mere automation and technological tools, encompassing the standardization of court operations, rationalizing processes, and developing regulatory frameworks that enable digital justice while protecting citizens’ rights to a fair trial, privacy, and security. Additionally, the Note highlights the importance of supporting all stakeholders in interacting with new digital technologies to build capacity and trust in digital justice systems, ensuring that these reforms are inclusive and effectively implemented.

display_title: Access to Information and Citizen Engagement in Governance in Cameroon
abstract: The Open Government Partnership (OGP) is a multilateral initiative launched in 2011 to promote open government. The initiative is based on principles that promote transparency and openness  to new forms of consultation, accountability, and collaboration with civil society. The OGP is based on the idea that open government is more accessible, responsive and accountable to citizens, and that improving the relationship between citizens and their government has long-term benefits for society as a whole. Governments are partnering with civil society (CS) to promote transparency, empower citizens, fight corruption, and improve their governance, including through the use of new technologies. This unique model of citizen engagement aims to ensure that CSOs play a role in shaping and citizen oversight of government action. IAA and EC are among the four criteria for membership in the Open Government Partnership (OGP). Cameroon is interested in becoming a member of the OGP but does not yet meet the required criteria. This diagnostic is intended to support the reforms undertaken by the government to improve its OGP eligibility. The IAA and CE report analyzes the strengths and weaknesses of open government in these two areas.

theme: Governance and Public Sector Management,Public Finance Management,Climate Change,Human Development,Environment, Infrastructure, and Natural Resource Management,Labor Markets Policy and Programs,Skills Development,Adaptation,Procurement,Mitigation
display_title: Status of Implementation of Lebanon’s Public Procurement Law 244-2021 and Assessment of Skills’ Gaps and Training Needs : Summary Report
abstract: Lebanon has been reforming its public procurement system since 2019. Public procurement reform is a key commitment of the Government of Lebanon (GoL) for structural fiscal reforms made at the Conférence économique pour le développement, par les réformes et avec les entreprises (CEDRE) in 2018, in Ministerial Statements (2019-2021) and in the Staff-Level Agreement with the IMF (2022). Following the Methodology for Assessing Procuring Systems (MAPS) assessment supported by the World Bank, Lebanon ratified the PPL 244/2021 in July 2021. The Law entered into force in July 2022. Concurrently, the National Strategy for Public Procurement Reform (NSPPR) and its 2022-2024 action plan were approved by the Council of Ministers in May 2022. This summary report presents the results of a qualitative survey conducted in 2023 by the Institut des Finances Basil Fuleihan (IOF), in collaboration with Insights Analytica, to assess the implementation of Lebanon’s Public Procurement Law (PPL 244-2021), 15 months after it enters into force. It aims to determine the status of the PPL 244/2021 implementation and assess skills gaps and specific training needs of procuring entities at the central and local levels of government towards an informed professionalization process. It also proposes recommendations to assist Lebanon in improving implementation practices, training, and capacity building, in line with PPL 244-2021 and the reform strategic vision. The survey’s findings offer insights into the effectiveness of the legal and institutional execution of PPL 244-2021, highlighting internal and external challenges, as well as training needs across procuring entities and proposing recommendations towards an effective implementation of PPL 244/2021 provisions.

theme: FY17 - Personal and Property Rights,FY17 - E-Government, incl. e-services,FY17 - Gender,FY17 - Public Administration,FY17 - Legal Institutions for a Market Economy,FY17 - Judicial and other Dispute Resolution Mechanisms,FY17 - Rule of Law
display_title: Azerbaijan - Judicial Services and Smart Infrastructure Project
abstract: Ratings for the Judicial Services and Smart Infrastructure Project for Azerbaijan were as follows: outcomes were satisfactory, the bank performance was also satisfactory, and the borrower's performance was also satisfactory. Some lessons learned included: other financing instruments, such as a Program-for-Results with disbursement-linked indicators (introduced in 2012) or an IPF with performance-based conditions, which could have been explored to engage and support the medium, to long-term vision for the sector. The Presidential Decrees of 2014 and 2019 underline the project and emphasize the need for technical upgrades and investments. However, they also reflect on Azerbaijan's commitment to an efficient justice system that supports socio-economic progress, as did the ‘Vision 2020’ and presented an opportunity to engage in policy dialogue and explore opportunities for a Program-for-Results. This could have benefitted activities that received less attention and struggled to fully meet expectations, such as support for JA in advancing judicial skills. The PIU struggled to balance the complexity and multitude of IT procurement vis-a-vis market limitations. The country office-based procurement team provided close support and multiple trainings while implementing JMP and JSSIP. The market research carried out at the beginning of the AF stage led to improvements in procurement packaging and resulted in more diversified contract awards. However, this did not overcome the country's limited IT capacity. A single vendor was heavily involved in follow-up system development contracts after the first system development contract was awarded to them, effectively positioning this vendor as the IT partner of the MoJ.

theme: Governance and Public Sector Management,Subnational Fiscal Policies,Public Finance Management,Climate Change,Economic Growth,Environment, Infrastructure, and Natural Resource Management,Economic Policy,Tax policy,Fiscal Policy,Mitigation,Domestic Revenue Administration
display_title: Ukraine: 2024 Tax Compliance Cost Survey - Report on Results of a Survey
abstract:
The Ukraine Taxpayer Compliance Cost Survey (TCCS) provides a snapshot of taxpayers’ experience filing and paying taxes and estimates of the cost of taxpayers’ compliance with State Tax Service (STS) administrative requirements and procedures related to tax accounting, including filing and paying taxes, working with tax inspectors, and filing appeals. The survey methodology is based on the standardized taxpayer compliance cost survey methodology developed by the World Bank. The
survey results provide a baseline against which future STS modernization efforts can be measured. The survey was web-based, targeting all active taxpayers registered with STS. 3,169 taxpayers responded to the survey. 85.1 percent of respondents were business owners, 74.1 percent were sole proprietors, and 25.9 percent were representatives of legal entities. 60.2 percent of businesses have no employees, 37.2 percent employ up to 50 people, and 2.6 percent have over 50 employees. 90 percent of businesses were in the service sector, 6.02 percent were in industry and 3.71 percent in agriculture. Survey responses came from all regions of Ukraine, except Crimea region.

theme: FY17 - Health Finance,FY17 - Gender,FY17 - Health Systems and Policies,FY17 - Health Service Delivery
display_title: Zambia - Emergency Health Service Delivery Project
abstract: Ratings for the Emergency Health Service Delivery Project for Zambia were as follows: outcomes were highly satisfactory, the bank performance was satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was high. Some lessons learned included: integrating development partners' financing into Zambia's PFM systems, which can enhance expenditure management and reduce administrative burdens. Reliance on separate, off-PFM systems has led to fragmented oversight, duplication of funding to similar activities, coordination challenges with Zambia’s health strategy, and reduced transparency in funds tracking. Channeling more external financing through the Treasury Single Account and standardizing reporting can enhance financial control, resource allocation, and the impact of health financing on strengthening Zambia’s health sector. Effective coordination at different government and health department levels was crucial for successfully implementing the project. Integrating IFMIS with health sector systems was crucial for effective expenditure tracking and accountability. Although initially planned, the integration was not implemented, leaving a gap in the intended improvements for monitoring non-salary recurrent costs and financial transparency in health sector funding. This integration would have enabled detailed tracking of expenses on supplies, maintenance, and other operational costs essential to the smooth functioning of health facilities. It will have have further provided real-time monitoring and reporting capabilities, allowing for the prompt identification and resolution of discrepancies, thus supporting financial discipline and reducing the risk of resource misallocation or misuse. Transparent financial practices also foster trust among stakeholders, including donors, government bodies, and the public, ensuring that funds are effectively used toward health sector objectives. Future engagement may need to prioritize integrating IFMIS with health sector systems to reinforce financial control, improve resource allocation, and enhance the impact of health financing on service delivery and institutional strengthening within the sector.

display_title: Gambia - Fiscal Management Development Project
abstract: Ratings for the Fiscal Management Development Project for the Gambia were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: governance operations, which by nature contribute to multi-dimensional goals and can be difficult to frame and measure from an M&E perspective. While FMDP contributed towards increasing fiscal space and improving the effectiveness of public resources management, both outcomes are substantially broad, continuous, and slow maturing, and cannot be singularly influenced by discrete project activities. Fiscal space is also prone to exogenous factors. In these cases, Governance teams are advised to conduct robust results chain mapping that can help to pinpoint outcomes reflective of and attributable to project contribution. More broadly, there would be significant value for the Governance Global Practice to invest in more practitioner exchange and thought around M&E that balances between ambition and attribution. This is a recurring challenge for operations contributing to DRM, fiscal management (including fiscal sustainability of the SOE sector), expenditure efficiency, citizen engagement and other governance technical streams. The Gambia is highly dependent on external financing with grants equivalent to 8.1 percent of GDP, driven by budget and project support. A consequence of this is a proliferation of PIUs and other parallel project structures, diluting government coordination and accountability, and contributing to keeping core capacities out of reach of the core public sector. Encouraged by the investments made in country systems by FMDP and by the performance of its PIU, noted for its strong engagement and effective collaboration with IEs, the World Bank, and other donors, the MoFEA established a Central Project Coordination Unit (CPCU) from the FMDP PIU. The FMDP PIU was uniquely qualified to take on this role given its demonstrated track record of coordinating across the multiple implementing entities involved in FMDP. The CPCU, embedded within MoFEA, currently manages several donor operations. With further investment in its resources, the CPCU can take on more operations with the twin benefit of rationalizing parallel systems and embedding the management of donor operations within government structures and systems.

display_title: Western Balkans - Policy Note Summarizing the WeBA Regional Stock-take for Local Government and Local Service Delivery
abstract: This document collection explores the challenges and opportunities facing local governments in a region. It examines how to improve data platforms and user engagement, the impact of decentralization on local governance, and the role of digital transformation in public service delivery. The collection also considers the requirements for regional policy and the development of a framework for effective local government service delivery.

display_title: Agreed Upon Procedures Report on the Public Expenditures for  Administrative Capacity Endurance  (PEACE) in Ukraine Project
abstract: Audit firm's PricewaterhouseCoopers report is solely for the purpose of assisting The World Bank Group and, as applicable, the Ministry of Finance of Ukraine in achieving the following objectives, stated below, in respect of the expenditures, reported to The World Bank for reimbursement under the Public Expenditures for Administrative Capacity Endurance (PEACE) in Ukraine  project (hereinafter – the Project) for the period from October 2023 to March 2024 to the extent financing has been provided for each category of expenditures (referred to as Phase II FY24), with a follow up on the periods from March to August 2022 (hereinafter “Phase I FY23”), from September to December 2022 (hereinafter “Phase II FY23”), from January to September 2023 (hereinafter “Phase I FY24”), and may not be suitable for another purpose: (1) Objective 1: Determine whether monthly reports on eligible government expenditures were correctly compiled and consolidated on the basis of reports received from lower-level units, and financial data included in such  reports reconciles with Treasury data or other applicable data sources; (2) Objective 2: Determine whether Government has functioning controls over preparation and compilation of such monthly eligible government expenditure reports, including exclusion of items that are financed by the government but not eligible to be financed by the Project; determine whether Government has functioning controls over accrual, payment and subsequent recording of those eligible government expenditures; (3) Objective 3: Determine for a sample of eligible government expenditures included in each such monthly report,  that those expenditures are correctly stated, and eligible, and such expenditures have been made in accordance with the project documents and Project Operating Manual.

theme: NA
display_title: Climate Change Framework Legislation : A Growing Trend
abstract: Countries are increasingly adopting climate change framework laws to help them achieve their climate goals. Since 1998 - when the first law was adopted - 61 countries (and one regional bloc, the European Union) have enacted framework laws. Half of these have been in the last five years. Framework laws are especially prevalent in the Americas, Western Europe, and the Pacific and are more common among high-income and upper-middle-income countries. They are least common in Africa and the Middle East. This note analyzes 12 key elements of framework laws, covering strategic goals, policy development and execution, evaluation and enforcement. The most frequently included elements are mandates for measurement, reporting, and verification, and strategies and plans; the least common are mandates for national and sectoral targets and risk and vulnerability assessments. This note provides an analytical structure and summary of the latest practice to support countries interested in enacting climate change framework laws.

display_title: Air Quality Management in EU Member States
abstract:
The objective of this technical note is to inform the governments of Bulgaria, Croatia, Poland,
and Romania about ongoing efforts and opportunities to strengthen AQM governance to
achieve better air quality in these countries. To this end, the note develops a framework by
which to assess institutional capacity for effective and efficient AQM. The framework consists
of 16 components organized around five attributes and a set of guidance questions to carry
out the assessment.

display_title: Nigeria - States Fiscal Transparency, Accountability, and Sustainability - Program-for-Results Project
abstract: Ratings of States Fiscal Transparency, Accountability, and Sustainability - Program-for-Results Project for Nigeria were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: direct and continuous engagement of the Bank with all the 36 states directly at all stages from design to implementation was a critical success factor of the program; an understanding of the political economy of individual states during the preparation phase of the program was key to design and to the team’s approach to engaging with individual states during implementation; while the program put in place the foundations for an institutionalized performance-based grant system, the program did not encompass measures that would have led to a more sustainable reform of the intergovernmental fiscal transfer system, that would have continued to incentivize the kind of fiscal management and transparency practices that were achieved during the lifetime of the program; a robust results verification protocol and process and credible IVA were key to build and maintain trust in the system from the states and the public; and more practitioners’ thought and exchange around methods and tools for measuring the success and failure of citizen participation efforts would have been beneficial.

display_title: India - Assam State Public Finance Institutional Reforms Project
abstract: Ratings of Assam State Public Finance Institutional Reforms Project for India were as follows: outcome was highly satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was modest. Some of the lessons learned include: Political buy-in and support was essential for reforms; Senior leadership involvement and an empowered executive committee helps in providing strategic direction, but lack of adequate delegation delayed certain procurements; Investments in process automation ensure business continuity in times of crisis; Continuous stakeholder engagement and communication of ‘gains’ generates buy-in for reforms; Creating IT capacities is a fundamental requirement in technology-intensive programs; Institutional reforms and behavior changes are more challenging and need more time to become ingrained and internalized than IT-driven transformations; and Results-based financing was a suitable approach for PFM reform projects.

theme: FY17 - Administrative and Civil Service Reform,FY17 - Public Administration
display_title: Humility on the Journey of State-Rebuilding in Somalia
abstract: This paper strives to explain the disconnect between lessons from research literature and the relative rigidity of state-building approaches by practitioners attempting post-war reconstruction in fragile states. It argues that a social contract approach can better explain why manifest improvements in service delivery may not improve state legitimacy and suggests different options for post-war state-building. However, recognizing the limitations of the suggestions, it also advocates that humility should be at the heart of engagement in FCV countries.

theme: FY17 - Administrative and Civil Service Reform,FY17 - Public Administration
display_title: Toward Building Somalia’s Social Contract : State Affordability, Revenue Mobilization, and Service Delivery in a Nascent Federal State
abstract:
State building and state legitimacy in the states emerging from fragility are inseparable from the notion of social contract. In the countries where the youth only know statelessness—or a complete absence of state institutions—building a new social contract from the ground up becomes an even stronger imperative. What can citizens legitimately expect from the state? Should the state provide only the basics—security and observance of social order—or should it also be involved in the provision of broader public goods and services, such as infrastructure, clean water, public health, and education? These questions are inextricably linked to the questions
of what is affordable. Most fragile states, given the destruction of state institutions and processes, suffer from very low tax capacity and hence feeble revenue collection and rely disproportionately on often unpredictable donor funds. Yet the ability of the state to collect revenue is also linked to the fiscal bargain side of the social contract. For citizens, paying taxes hinges on the trust that public funds will be translated into key public goods and services. Higher trust means higher willingness to pay taxes.
Social contract is a dynamic agreement between the state and society, delineating mutual roles and responsibilities that are continuously renegotiated based on changing capacities and expectations. It is also understood as an implicit agreement where the state provides security and public goods in exchange for citizens’ compliance and support. This concept serves as a framework for understanding how states gain legitimacy, maintain social order, and deliver public goods such as justice, security, and development—supporting an understanding of the local context and the need for tailored approaches to strengthen the social contract in different contexts. While this volume examines the dynamics of building Somalia’s social contract, it does not delve into the nature of the bargaining interfaces but rather explores the outcomes of the social contract, including the state’s capacity for revenue mobilization and service delivery through its multiple administrative units. Importantly, it provides information on how the alignment of expectations between the state and society and outcomes has evolved over the past few years.

theme: FY17 - Data production, accessibility and use,FY17 - Public Administration,FY17 - Data Development and Capacity Building,FY17 - Transparency, Accountability and Good Governance,FY17 - State-owned Enterprise Reform and Privatization
display_title: State-Owned Enterprises (SOES) in Oman : Review of SOE Governance Practices
abstract: This report is part of a World Bank review of Stateowned Enterprise (SOE) governance practices in Middle East and North Africa (MENA) countries. The review is responding to the scarcity of data about such practices in the MENA region. It initially covers six countries, including: Djibouti, Egypt, Jordan, Morocco, Oman, and Tunisia. The objective of the reviews is to develop and disseminate knowledge about SOE governance in the interests of promoting continued SOE reforms in the region. This report provides an overview of the SOE landscape and history in Oman, followed by a review of key dimensions of SOE governance practices. This includes a review of the following dimensions: (i) The legal and regulatory frameworks for SOEs; (ii) State ownership arrangements; (iii) Performance management frameworks; (iv) SOE board structures and functioning; (v) Transparency and disclosure practices; (vi) Procurement policies and practices; and (vii) Climate reporting practices. The framework for the review is based on the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) Guidelines for SOE Corporate Governance and the World Bank’s Integrated SOE Framework (iSOEF).

display_title: Better Investment Outcomes in Viet Nam through Increased Public Investment Management Efficiency
abstract:
Viet Nam’s long-term goal is to attain high-income status by 2045. Achieving a modern, industrialized and resilient economy with higher living standards will depend on well-designed government policy interventions, combined with well targeted, efficient and effective public expenditures. Among the latter, public investment will play a critical part in creating the environment for wealth creation and
social welfare improvements. However, it will not just be the volume of public investment that counts but also the quality, and this will become more important as time goes by. As diagnostic studies have shown, the quality of public investment is currently compromised by fragmentation, duplication and inefficiency. Overcoming these difficulties will be a major challenge in meeting Viet Nam’s development aspirations, which forms the broader context for the current Technical Report. In addressing the challenge of improving the quality of public investment, the Technical Report homes in on four potential hurdles that will need to be overcome: i) a complex and overlapping legal and regulatory framework for public investment management; ii) inefficiencies in managing the portfolio of
ongoing and planned investment projects through the medium-term investment plan; iii) the absence of a standardised methods and documents for assessing project quality prior to a funding decision; and iv) obstacles to mobilising official development assistance (ODA) in a timely way. The analysis in the Technical Report seeks to examine these four hurdles in depth and propose solutions.

display_title: Uzbekistan - Tax Administration Reform Project : Project Progress Report for the Period June-October 2024
abstract: The development objective of Tax Administration Reform Project for Uzbekistan is increasing the operational efficiency and effectiveness of the tax department and the level of voluntary compliance with tax legislation. This progress report covers the project activities for the period of June to October 2024.

theme: FY17 - Public Expenditure Management,FY17 - Public Finance Management,FY17 - Economic Growth and Planning,FY17 - Trade,FY17 - Trade Policy,FY17 - Structural Transformation and Economic Diversification
display_title: Drivers of Delays in Procurement of Infrastructure Projects
abstract: The objective of this study was to analyze the reasons for delays in procurement and contract execution of infrastructure projects. It analyzes available data from three different sources: Tender data for 656 works contracts extracted from the World Bank Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP), covering the period 2013-2022. This sample was used to analyze the incidence and extent of delays in the procurement of World Bank infrastructure projects; A sample of 312 works contracts for infrastructure projects financed by the World Bank and the Islamic Development Bank (IsDB), covering the period 2007-2019. This sample was used to analyze the reasons for delays in the procurement and delivery of World Bank and IsDB infrastructure projects at the contract level; CoST assurance reports from 2016 to 2020 covering 480 infrastructure projects across 12 member countries of CoST. This sample was used to analyze the reasons of delays for the reviewed infrastructure projects.

display_title: PEFA++ Colombia 2024 : Evaluación del Desempeño de la Gestión de las Finanzas Públicas de Colombia - Informe Final
abstract: El ejercicio PEFA++ Colombia 2024 tiene su origen en la nota enviada por el Ministerio de Hacienda y Crédito Público (MHCP) al Banco Mundial (BM), la Agencia Francesa de Desarrollo (AFD) y la Unión Europea (UE) para solicitar su apoyo con la preparación e implementación de una nueva evaluación PEFA del sistema de gestión de las finanzas públicas en el país. En su nota, el Ministro de Hacienda y Crédito Público afirma que esta evaluación permitiría valorar la situación actual de desempeño de este sistema, además de conocer el impacto de los esfuerzos de reforma que se han venido desarrollando desde la anterior evaluación en 2016, incluyendo aquellas iniciativas orientadas a dar soporte a la implementación de las políticas de igualdad de género y de cambio climático. El ejercicio PEFA++ Colombia 2024 se inició formalmente el 1 de abril de 2024 con actividades de preparación general y organización de la misión de campo. La misión de campo se desarrolló entre el 6 de mayo y el 7 de junio de 2024 y se inició con el lanzamiento formal de la evaluación por parte del MHCP en una reunión en la que participaron autoridades del Gobierno de Colombia y representantes de las agencias de cooperación internacional que respaldan el ejercicio. El taller de formación sobre la metodología PEFA se llevó a cabo entre el 7 y 8 de mayo de 2024, evento en el que se presentaron, además del Marco PEFA 2016, los marcos complementarios PEFA Género y PEFA Clima. Durante las siguientes tres semanas de la misión de campo se desarrollaron reuniones con las autoridades y funcionarios de todas las entidades vinculadas a la evaluación y se recogió la información y documentación necesaria. Al final de la misión, el Equipo de Evaluación hizo una presentación de los resultados preliminares de la evaluación al Comité de Supervisión.

theme: FY17 - Public Administration,FY17 - ICT Solutions,FY17 - ICT
display_title: World Bank GovTech Operations in Jordan: A Digital Transformation Case Study
abstract: GovTech is a whole-of-government approach that promotes simple, efficient and transparent government with the citizen at the center of reforms. While earlier e-government programs focused on building IT systems for public administration and moving services online that often remained analog in design, GovTech strategies aim to provide citizen-centric services that are digital by design and coordinated across the government, and it prioritizes two-way interaction with citizens by mainstreaming citizen engagement.

theme: NA
display_title: Nepal - Public Financial Management Multi-donor Trust Fund Progress Report : January - June 2024
abstract: Strengthening Public Financial Management (PFM) represents a critical aspect of the Government of Nepal’s (GON) strategy to improve governance, optimize the utilization of public resources, and promote inclusive development. There has been increasing interest from civil society organizations, media, the public, and development partners (DPs) in strengthening PFM to enhance the use of public resources and achieve value for money. To this end, the GON has been working closely with DPs to design and implement PFM reform initiatives at the country and sector levels. In response to the GON’s and DPs’ call for a coordinated approach to address PFM challenges, the Nepal PFM Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF) was established to harmonize DPs’ support for PFM reforms.

theme: FY17 - Public Expenditure Management,FY17 - Public Administration,FY17 - Public Finance Management,FY17 - Transparency, Accountability and Good Governance
display_title: Making it All Count : Strengthening the Independence of the Audit Function in Liberia
abstract: This assessment has been undertaken to review the Independence of the General Auditing Commission (GAC) and how it may affect the effectiveness of the external audit function in Liberia and to support the Government in strengthening the audit institution's performance and safeguarding the use of public funds. Over the past decade, Liberia has implemented significant public financial management (PFM) reforms to enhance public funds' efficiency, accountability, and transparency. Reforms include enacting a new PFM Act in 2019 and implementing the Integrated Financial Management Information System (IFMIS) and the Integrated Tax Administration System (ITAS). However, a long journey of PFM reforms is ahead, as evidenced by several assessments such as the Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability (PEFA) scores and the World Bank’s Country Policy and Institutional Assessment (CPIA) ratings.1 On the latter, Liberia has consistently scored just 2.5 out of a total of 6 points covering the areas of budgetary and financial management quality, transparency, accountability, and corruption in the public sector since 2018. The assessment is based on global evidence that the extent of the SAI's independence is crucial for reducing waste and misusing public resources. The assessment has ten indicators that measure different aspects of independence: legal, financial, mandate, coverage, and operational dimensions. After reviewing the evidence, each indicator is rated from 0 to 1 based on the assessor's expert opinion.

display_title: Green Competitiveness in Ethiopia : An Overview of How Environmental and Climate Factors Increasingly Shape Ethiopia's Economic Outlook in Selected Value Chains
abstract: Environmental and climate factors play an increasing role in shaping Ethiopia’s economic competitiveness, and this report aims to provide an overview of these shifts. This novel report is a high-level assessment of how certain factors could affect Ethiopia’s economic competitiveness: (i) supply side impacts of climate change and environmental degradation, and (ii) demand-side changes caused by a growing number of sustainability requirements in key export markets, notably sustainability regulations and decisions by international buyers. Concentrating on four sectors that are both critical to Ethiopia’s economy and exposed to environmental and climate factors - coffee, textiles and garments, cut flowers, and aviation - illustrates these shifts. The objective is to identify cross-cutting trends of how sustainability factors affect Ethiopia’s economic competitiveness, but the sector-specific angle helps identify pressing challenges that policy makers in Ethiopia need to address. Note that the selected sectors are used to illustrate the trends described in this report and do not imply a recommended prioritization. Many other sectors essential to Ethiopia’s green transformation are not discussed. Moreover, although the report acknowledges that social and environmental aspects are deeply intertwined, it does not cover topics such as occupational health and safety, inclusion, living wages, and gender rights. The assessment applies a mixed methods approach by drawing on insights from interviews with experts conducted online and in person in Ethiopia (conducted mainly between November 2023 and April 2024), analysis of trade and economic data, and an extensive literature review. This report underlines the macro criticality of green competitiveness for Ethiopia, embedded in the wider economic and political context.

display_title: Poland - Reimbursable Advisory Services Agreement on Supporting Poland in the Implementation of its State Purchasing Policy : Deliverable 1b - Report on Dissemination and Communication of the Guidelines for the Competency Framework
abstract: The public procurement function plays a key role in meeting the European Union’s (EU) strategic goal of becoming the most competitive and dynamic knowledge-based economy in the world and securing the benefits of sustainable economic growth and social cohesion for Poland, in addition to ensuring value for taxpayers’ money. As part of its Strategy for Public Procurement (PP), the European Commission (EC) issued the recommendation on the professionalization of public procurement in 2017 which highlights the need for adoption of professionalization policies to support the capacity development of contracting authorities. At the same time, ProcurCompEU - the European competency framework for public procurement professionals was developed to support the professionalization of public procurement. In line with the European Commission’s recommendation on the professionalization of public procurement, the Polish State Purchasing Policy (SPP) recognizes professionalization of public procurement as one of its key objectives. This report provides insights on the ProcurCompEU implementation in Poland based on the interactions with and feedback from selected contracting authorities during the preparation, refinement, and dissemination of the ProcurCompEU guidelines and communication brief as part of the Reimbursable Advisory Service (RAS) Agreement between the World Bank and the Polish Government. The report corresponds to activity (iii) of Component 1 Professionalization.

display_title: Poland - RAS Agreement on Supporting Poland in the Implementation of its State Purchasing Policy : Deliverable 3 - Report on the Baseline Assessment of Micro-Small and Medium Enterprise Participation in Public Procurement
abstract: The public procurement function plays a key role in meeting the strategic goal of the European Union (EU) to become the most competitive and dynamic knowledge-based economy in the world and to secure the benefits of sustainable economic growth and social cohesion, in addition to ensuring value for taxpayers’ money. The European Union established specific criteria for the classification of MSMEs based on employment numbers and financial thresholds. Reforms that broaden access to public procurement contracts can be a key growth opportunity for Poland’s MSMEs, with important spillovers for the country’s economy. In line with the EU public procurement strategy, the Polish State Purchasing Policy (SPP) for the period 2022- 2025 sets out the ambition to change the role of public procurement from an administrative function to a strategic enabler, by taking action in three main priority areas. To support MSMEs, the 2019 Public Procurement Law and the Polish SPP implement measures to reduce barriers to their participation and ensure equitable access to public contracts. This report proposes several areas where further action would be helpful to increase MSME participation.

theme: NA
display_title: Reflections on the Functional Review of the Romanian Justice Sector
abstract: The Functional Review of the Romanian Justice Sector (JFR), conducted through a World Bank Reimbursable Advisory Services (RAS) agreement, represented a significant effort by the Government of Romania to assess and enhance the performance and management of the justice sector. Spanning two years, the review aimed to comprehensively evaluate the sector’s functioning, identify areas for improvement, and provide recommendations for improved service delivery. This document captures key observations and insights on Romania’s justice sector, drawing on the findings and conclusions of the JFR, following RAS completion. While the focus is on Romania’s ongoing justice reform, the document also aims to distill broader conclusions and lessons that are relevant to the wider international context, as justice systems globally face evolving challenges and demands. The ultimate goal is to stimulate dialogue on how justice sectors can adapt to new societal needs, emphasizing justice as a fundamental, universally accessible public good that underpins a just and stable society. The comprehensive approach of the JFR offers valuable insights for justice systems globally, making the findings and recommendations relevant beyond Romania.

display_title: Congo, Republic of - Integrated Public Sector Reform Project
abstract: Ratings of Integrated Public Sector Reform Project for Congo, Republic of were as follows: outcome was satisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some of the lessons learned include: The implementation schedule of governance programs should be sufficiently long and flexible; Proper establishment and capacitation of the Project Coordination Unit (PCU) with clear institutional setup and labor division before effectiveness is critical; World Bank project leads should ideally be based in country to make them easily accessible to PCU/Project Implementation Unit (PIUs); High-level political commitment and champions of reform are vital elements for the project's success but can often be a vulnerability; For a low-capacity environment, from the onset, the project design should be simple and easy to follow with a clearly defined scope and theory of change; and On the rollout of digital systems, the project design should be comprehensive from end to end and strictly target the achievement of the ultimate outcomes and not outputs.

display_title: Cameroon - Strengthening Public Sector Effectiveness and Statistical Capacity Project
abstract: Ratings for the Strengthening Public Sector Effectiveness and Statistical Capacity Project for Cameroon were as follows: outcome was moderately unsatisfactory, Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was modest. Some lessons learned included: reducing the time lag between project approval and effectiveness is critical. The design of appropriate institutional arrangements that define clear roles and responsibilities between the Project Coordination Unit (PCU) staff and the focal points of the beneficiaries is also important in fragile, conflict-affected and vulnerable (FCV) settings. In FCV settings such as Cameroun, task teams should be encouraged to simplify project design to have limited and focused areas of intervention, even when faced with many needs and requests from the country. M and E should be an integral part of a project's operations manual, and the World Bank (WB) team should monitor its effectiveness; M and E should also be capable of producing adequate and timely data for project management. The process for selection of PCU staff at entry and regular performance evaluation throughout the project needs to be strengthened. A field-based task team leader can be instrumental for successful supervision of Governance projects in Cameroon and similar FCV countries or projects with complex design and many beneficiaries and areas of intervention.

display_title: Bangladesh - Digitizing Implementation Monitoring and Public Procurement Project
abstract: Ratings for the Digitizing Implementation Monitoring and Public Procurement Project for Bangladesh were as follows: outcomes were moderately satisfactory, the Bank performance was moderately satisfactory, and the monitoring and evaluation quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: first, strong and consistent signaling from political leadership is crucially important for difficult systems reforms like this one where there is potential for rent seeking. Second, project reporting with candor is more likely to lead to course adjustment that improves results. Third, if the Theory of Change is outlined clearly, comprehensively, and explicitly, it can be understood and applied more rapidly to other country situations.  A fourth lesson learned, not substantially different from those in previous projects, is a part of design that is worth repeating. The type and extent of training is important to a systems development project, for effective uptake and usage. Extensive training is a key to success. Supporting this broad training, a helpdesk set up by the project was important for real-time assistance. This must be adequately staffed, with specialists for tricky issues and continue even after the project closing. In the DIMAPPP-AF, there was a focus on developing not just trainers to deliver future trainings but also institutions and training centers that could provide training in the future.

theme: FY17 - Judicial and other Dispute Resolution Mechanisms,FY17 - Rule of Law
display_title: Strengthening justice for business in Serbia - Commercial Courts Needs Assessment
abstract: The functioning of the judiciary is one of the most significant constraints for doing business in Serbia, as evidenced by both international reports and surveys of the business sector. A well-functioning judiciary is of a crucial importance for the functioning of a market economy, as it ensures the protection of property rights and the enforcement of contracts. Even though effective legal protection is important for all businesses it is of particular importance for micro, small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs). Their  relative power imbalance vis-à-vis other market players makes them less capable of equitably resolving disputes and upholding their rights. MSMEs encounter unique challenges that make them particularly reliant on an effective judiciary. MSMEs often operate with tight budgets, making it difficult to afford prolonged legal disputes or expensive legal representation. Protracted legal disputes can divert critical time and resources away from the core business operations, hindering growth and innovation. Unlike large corporations, MSMEs often lack dedicated legal departments which makes them more vulnerable to legal complexities and procedural errors. Considering that MSMEs represent 99 percent of the Serbian economy, the importance of a functional judiciary is further amplified.

abstract: Ratings for the Program to Strengthen Governance for Enabling Service Delivery and Public Investment in Kenya (GESDeK) for Kenya were as follows: outcome and Bank performance was satisfactory and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was substantial. Some lessons learned included: in designing disbursement linked indicator (DLIs), it is important to avoid indicators that depend on the legislative approval process. Opting for fewer focused DLIs has the potential to enable higher disbursements and greater achievement of strategic outcomes. Quality of technical design complemented by adequate financing and technical assistance (TA) were critical success factors in the achievement of results. Alignment of the program-for-results (PforR) Program to reflect evolving government priorities was critical.

display_title: Digitalization for Anticorruption  - World Bank’s recommendations on the way forward
abstract: This note analyzes the current state of digitalization in Armenia for corruption prevention and provides inputs and recommendations to inform the ongoing strategic planning process in anticorruption led by the Ministry of Justice (MoJ) and supported by the Commission for Prevention of Corruption (CPC).  This note focuses on the digitalization efforts and needs for the CPC to perform its main functions. It also provides recommendations which could be relevant for other key anticorruption actors in Armenia and that would result in efficiency gains from potential technology and data sharing and an effective anti-corruption strategy. It highlights cross-cutting issues of digital governance that affect the anticorruption activities and are outside of the CPC’s mandate, for which significant coordination and collaboration among stakeholders will be required.
